title: A polarized beam passes through two polarizing filters
stem: A beam of polarized light with intensity I₀ = 240 W/m² first passes through a polarizing filter whose axis is 35° from the beam's polarization direction, then through a second filter whose axis is 70° from the first filter's axis.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Find the intensity after the first filter.

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Find the intensity after the second filter.

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: What fraction of the original intensity survives both filters? Does the order of the filters matter?
parts.answer: I₂/I₀ = cos²(35°)cos²(70°) ≈ 0.0785, about 7.85%. Order matters: each filter applies Malus's Law using the angle between itself and the polarization it receives, so swapping the filters would give cos²(70°)cos²(35°) — the same total, but intermediate intensities differ.

title: At what angle does a polarizer cut intensity to exactly one-third?
stem: A polarized light beam of intensity I₀ passes through a single polarizing filter.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: At what angle θ (to the nearest tenth of a degree) must the polarizer's axis be set so that the transmitted intensity is exactly I₀/3?

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: A student argues: 'Since cos(60°) = 0.5, the intensity at 60° should be I₀/2.' Identify the error in this reasoning.
parts.answer: The student confused E-field amplitude with intensity. Malus's Law gives I = I₀cos²θ (intensity proportional to field squared), not I = I₀cosθ. At θ = 60°: I = I₀cos²(60°) = I₀(0.5)² = I₀/4, not I₀/2.

title: Sunlight through a polarizing window film
stem: A window is coated with a vertical-axis polarizing film. Sunlight (unpolarized, I₀ = 1050 W/m²) enters the window and then passes through a handheld polarizing filter that a student rotates to different angles θ measured from vertical.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: What is the intensity of light immediately after passing through the window film, before the handheld filter?
parts.answer: The window film polarizes unpolarized light, so intensity is halved: I_window = I₀/2 = 525 W/m²

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: The student holds the handheld filter at θ = 40° from vertical. What intensity do they measure?

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: The student rotates to θ = 90°. What happens, and why does this make polarized sunglasses useful for looking at LCD screens?
parts.answer: At θ = 90°: I = 525 × cos²(90°) = 0. Complete blocking. LCD screens emit polarized light; rotating polarized sunglasses to 90° from the screen's polarization axis blacks it out — useful for reducing glare or improving contrast on certain screen orientations.

title: A train moves east at 20 m/s relative to the ground. A passe
stem: A train moves east at 20 m/s relative to the ground. A passenger on the train throws a ball forward (east) at 5 m/s relative to themselves.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: What is the velocity of the ball as measured by an observer standing on the ground?

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: If a second observer is moving west at 10 m/s relative to the ground, what velocity do they measure for the ball?

title: Two events occur in spacetime. Event A is at position x = 0
stem: Two events occur in spacetime. Event A is at position x = 0 light-seconds at time t = 0 seconds. Event B is at position x = 5 light-seconds at time t = 3 seconds.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Calculate the spacetime interval Δs² between Event A and Event B using the formula Δs² = −Δt² + Δx².

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Is the separation between these two events space-like, time-like, or light-like? Justify your answer using the value of Δs² you found in part (a).

title: Space Cows -- mooo!
stem: Bessie and Charli are two space cows traveling to the right through deep space. Farmer Frank, at rest on his space station, observes Bessie traveling to the right at 0.90c and Charli traveling to the right at 0.70c.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Using the relativistic velocity addition formula, calculate the velocity of Bessie as observed by Charli in Charli's own rest frame. Express your answer in terms of c.

title: An astronaut travels from Earth to Alpha Centauri (4.37 ligh
stem: An astronaut travels from Earth to Alpha Centauri (4.37 light-years away) in a spaceship moving at β = 4/5 (i.e., v = 0.8c).

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: How long does the trip take according to an observer on Earth?

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: How long does the trip take according to the astronaut on the spaceship?

title: Thre relativity of nile boats.
stem: A boat travels down the Nile River. The river current flows at v = 3 m/s north relative to the ground (frame S). The boat moves at u = 7 m/s north relative to the ground (frame S).

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: What is the boat's velocity u′ as measured in the reference frame S′ of the river current?
